message.content:
You are an independent security auditor doing a FOCUSED break-test of one function. READ-ONLY: no edits, no git, no gh. Target: `require_local_uri` in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_local.py on branch `feat/persistence-port`. It enforces ADR-0003 data sovereignty: any store endpoint must be loopback-only. Two prior fixes of the SAME bypass class were incomplete, so assume nothing.

Your ONLY job: try as hard as you can to make `require_local_uri(<input>, what="x")` ACCEPT (not raise) an endpoint that actually points at a REMOTE host. Use /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python to run real repros against the function. Also, where relevant, check what `psycopg`/libpq would actually connect to for the same string (reason from the PostgreSQL connection-string spec) so an "accepted" input that resolves remotely is a real finding.

Attack surface to probe exhaustively:
- libpq keyword/value DSN host spellings: whitespace/tabs/newlines around '=', repeated `host=` keys (does libpq use the LAST? does the guard check ALL?), `host=localhost host=db.evil.com` and the reverse, quoted values with escaped quotes (`host='db.evil\'.com'`), backslash escapes, empty `host=''` then `hostaddr=8.8.8.8`, `hostaddr` vs `host` precedence, uppercase/mixed `HoSt`, unicode lookalikes, trailing/leading spaces, a value containing '=' or spaces when quoted, comment-like junk.
- URI forms: `postgresql://[REDACTED]@db.evil.com/x` (userinfo vs host), `postgresql://db.evil.com:5432/x?host=localhost`, IPv6 `[::1]` vs `[::ffff:1.2.3.4]` vs `[::ffff:127.0.0.1]`, decimal/octal/hex IP (`2130706433`, `0x7f000001`, `017700000001`), `127.0.0.1.evil.com`, trailing dot `localhost.`, `0.0.0.0`, `localhost%2eevil.com`, port tricks.
- scheme-relative `//host`, `////`, and mixed `sqlite:///x?host=evil`.
- Anything that makes urlparse and libpq DISAGREE about the host (parser differential) — that's the highest-value bug.

For EACH input you try, report: input, ACCEPT/REJECT by the function, and where libpq/the real client would connect. Then list any GENUINE bypass (accepted-but-remote) as FINDING N — SEVERITY / the input / why it's remote / SUGGESTED FIX. If you cannot find any bypass after a thorough attempt, say so explicitly and summarize the classes you tried and why they're safe. Do not pad.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
feat/persistence-port
4233f5b fix(persistence): close residual DSN loopback bypass via whitespace/quoting (D1)
534ef94 fix(persistence): resolve 3 audit findings (DSN loopback bypass, __context__ leak, coverage)
3813959 fix(persistence): restore the hardened checkpointer + document the design (ADR-0006/0007)

1	"""Data-sovereignty helper: enforce that network endpoints are loopback-only (ADR-0003).
2	
3	Every engine component that takes a host/URL for an on-box service (local model runtime,
4	tracing collector, vector store, checkpoint database) routes it through here so a non-local
5	address fails loud rather than silently shipping data off the machine.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	import ipaddress
11	import re
12	from urllib.parse import urlparse
13	
14	# A libpq keyword/value token: ``key = value`` with optional whitespace around ``=`` (libpq
15	# permits it) and an optionally single/double-quoted value (which may contain spaces).
16	_DSN_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"(\w+)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
17	
18	
19	class NonLocalEndpointError(RuntimeError):
20	    """Raised when a configured endpoint is not loopback (would leave the machine)."""
21	
22	
23	def is_loopback_host(host: str | None) -> bool:
24	    """True only for genuine loopback hosts.
25	
26	    Parses the host as an IP and tests the loopback network (127.0.0.0/8, ::1), so a public
27	    name that merely *starts with* ``127.`` (e.g. ``127.0.0.1.evil.com``) is correctly
28	    rejected. The literal ``localhost`` is the only accepted non-IP host.
29	    """
30	    if not host:
31	        return False
32	    h = host.strip().lower()
33	    if h == "localhost":
34	        return True
35	    try:
36	        return ipaddress.ip_address(h.strip("[]")).is_loopback
37	    except ValueError:
38	        return False
39	
40	
41	def require_loopback_url(url: str, *, what: str) -> str:
42	    """Return ``url`` if its host is loopback; else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`."""
43	    host = urlparse(url).hostname
44	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
45	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
46	            f"{what} must be loopback-only (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got host {host!r} "
47	            f"from {url!r}"
48	        )
49	    return url
50	
51	
52	def require_loopback_host(host: str, *, what: str) -> str:
53	    """Return ``host`` if it is loopback; else raise. (``0.0.0.0`` binds all interfaces and
54	    is correctly rejected — it would expose data off-box.)"""
55	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
56	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
57	            f"{what} must be a loopback host (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got {host!r}"
58	        )
59	    return host
60	
61	
62	def require_local_uri(uri: str, *, what: str) -> str:
63	    """Return ``uri`` if it is local (ADR-0003), else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`.
64	
65	    Three forms are recognized:
66	
67	    - **URI with a host component** (``scheme://host…`` or scheme-relative ``//host…``): the
68	      host must be loopback. A networked host smuggled into a ``sqlite://host`` or ``//host``
69	      spelling is rejected; a host-less ``sqlite:///file`` or ``file:///path`` is local.
70	    - **libpq keyword/value DSN** (no URI host but contains ``key=value`` tokens, e.g.
71	      ``host=db port=5432 dbname=x``): the ``host``/``hostaddr`` token must be loopback. This
72	      form has no ``://`` and would otherwise slip past a URI-only check (audit issue #P1).
73	    - **bare path** (no URI host, no ``=``): a local file/dir — local.
74	    """
75	    host = (urlparse(uri).hostname or "").strip()
76	    if host:
77	        if not is_loopback_host(host):
78	            raise NonLocalEndpointError(
79	                f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got host {host!r}"
80	            )
81	        return uri
82	    if "=" in uri:  # libpq keyword/value DSN (no URI host component)
83	        # Whitespace is permitted around '=' in libpq DSNs (e.g. "host = db.evil.com"), so a
84	        # naive token split misses the host value — a real tokenizer is required (audit #D1).
85	        for match in _DSN_TOKEN_RE.finditer(uri):
86	            if match.group(1).strip().lower() in {"host", "hostaddr"}:
87	                h = match.group(2).strip().strip("'\"")
88	                if h and not is_loopback_host(h):
89	                    raise NonLocalEndpointError(
90	                        f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); DSN names "
91	                        f"non-loopback host {h!r}"
92	                    )
93	        return uri
94	    return uri  # bare path / dir store
95

content.text:
Now I understand the function. Let me analyze the key logic and build a comprehensive test harness. The critical insight: the function first tries `urlparse(uri).hostname`. If that yields a host, it checks that. If no host but `=` present, it tokenizes as a DSN. The highest-value bugs are parser differentials between `urlparse` and what libpq actually does.

Let me write a comprehensive repro script.

content.text: `psql` is available — that links against the real libpq. I can use `PQconninfoParse` indirectly, but better: I can use Python's ctypes to call libpq's `PQconninfoParse` directly to get the GROUND TRUTH of what libpq resolves `host`/`hostaddr` to. Let me locate libpq.
I have real libpq. Let me write a harness that uses ctypes to call `PQconninfoParse` for ground truth on DSN strings, plus tests the function directly, plus uses urlparse for differentials.

input.content:
import sys, ctypes
from urllib.parse import urlparse
sys.path.insert(0, "/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src")
from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri, NonLocalEndpointError

# --- ground truth from real libpq ---
lib = ctypes.CDLL("/opt/homebrew/lib/postgresql@17/libpq.dylib")

class PQconninfoOption(ctypes.Structure):
    _fields_ = [
        ("keyword", ctypes.c_char_p),
        ("envvar", ctypes.c_char_p),
        ("compiled", ctypes.c_char_p),
        ("val", ctypes.c_char_p),
        ("label", ctypes.c_char_p),
        ("dispchar", ctypes.c_char_p),
        ("dispsize", ctypes.c_int),
    ]

lib.PQconninfoParse.restype = ctypes.POINTER(PQconninfoOption)
lib.PQconninfoParse.argtypes = [ctypes.c_char_p, ctypes.POINTER(ctypes.c_char_p)]
lib.PQconninfoFree.argtypes = [ctypes.POINTER(PQconninfoOption)]

def libpq_target(dsn):
    """Return what libpq resolves (host, hostaddr) to, or ('ERR', errmsg)."""
    errp = ctypes.c_char_p()
    opts = lib.PQconninfoParse(dsn.encode(), ctypes.byref(errp))
    if not opts:
        msg = errp.value.decode() if errp.value else "parse error"
        return ("ERR", msg.strip())
    res = {}
    i = 0
    while opts[i].keyword:
        kw = opts[i].keyword.decode()
        if kw in ("host", "hostaddr") and opts[i].val:
            res[kw] = opts[i].val.decode()
        i += 1
    lib.PQconninfoFree(opts)
    return res

def check(uri, note=""):
    # function verdict
    try:
        require_local_uri(uri, what="x")
        verdict = "ACCEPT"
    except NonLocalEndpointError:
        verdict = "REJECT"
    except Exception as e:
        verdict = f"ERR({type(e).__name__}:{e})"
    up = urlparse(uri).hostname
    # libpq only meaningful for keyword/value DSN (no scheme) or URI
    lp = libpq_target(uri)
    print(f"[{verdict:7}] up.host={up!r:24} libpq={lp}  | {uri!r} {note}")

tests = [
    # repeated host keys
    ("host=localhost host=db.evil.com dbname=x", "repeated host, evil last"),
    ("host=db.evil.com host=localhost dbname=x", "repeated host, localhost last"),
    ("host=localhost hostaddr=8.8.8.8 dbname=x", "host local, hostaddr remote"),
    ("host='' hostaddr=8.8.8.8 dbname=x", "empty host, hostaddr remote"),
    ("hostaddr=8.8.8.8 dbname=x", "hostaddr only"),
    ("host = db.evil.com dbname=x", "spaces around ="),
    ("host\t=\tdb.evil.com dbname=x", "tabs around ="),
    ("host=db.evil.com\ndbname=x", "newline separator"),
    ("HoSt=db.evil.com dbname=x", "mixed case key"),
    ("host=DB.EVIL.COM dbname=x", "upper value"),
    ("host='db.evil.com' dbname=x", "single quoted"),
    ("host=\"db.evil.com\" dbname=x", "double quoted"),
    (r"host='db.evil\'.com' dbname=x", "escaped quote in value"),
    (r"host=db.evil\ .com dbname=x", "backslash escape space"),
    ("host=localhost\\ dbname=x", "trailing backslash on localhost"),
    ("host=127.0.0.1.evil.com dbname=x", "prefix trick"),
    ("host=localhost. dbname=x", "trailing dot"),
    ("host=0.0.0.0 dbname=x", "all-interfaces"),
    ("host=2130706433 dbname=x", "decimal IP"),
    ("host=0x7f000001 dbname=x", "hex IP"),
    ("host=017700000001 dbname=x", "octal IP"),
    # URI forms
    ("postgresql://[REDACTED]@db.evil.com/x", "userinfo vs host"),
    ("postgresql://db.evil.com:5432/x?host=localhost", "host in query"),
    ("postgresql://localhost/x?host=db.evil.com", "loopback host, evil in query"),
    ("postgresql://[::1]/x", "ipv6 loopback"),
    ("postgresql://[::ffff:1.2.3.4]/x", "ipv4-mapped remote"),
    ("postgresql://[::ffff:127.0.0.1]/x", "ipv4-mapped loopback"),
    ("postgresql://127.0.0.1.evil.com/x", "prefix host uri"),
    ("postgresql://localhost.evil.com/x", "localhost prefix uri"),
    ("sqlite:///x?host=db.evil.com", "sqlite query host"),
    ("sqlite://db.evil.com/x", "sqlite host"),
    ("//db.evil.com/x", "scheme-relative"),
    ("////x", "many slashes"),
    ("postgresql:///x?host=db.evil.com", "pg uri socket-ish, host in query"),
    ("postgresql:///x?hostaddr=8.8.8.8", "pg uri, hostaddr in query"),
    ("postgresql://@/x?host=db.evil.com", "empty authority, host query"),
    ("postgres://localhost/x", "postgres scheme"),
    ("host=localhost\x00host=db.evil.com", "null byte injection"),
    ("host=/tmp dbname=x", "unix socket dir"),
    ("dbname=x host=db.evil.com", "host not first"),
    ("host=db.evil.com", "no = besides host (still has =)"),
]

for t in tests:
    if isinstance(t, tuple):
        check(t[0], t[1])
    else:
        check(t)

content.text:
I found a critical hit. The line:

```
[ACCEPT] up.host='localhost'  libpq={'host': 'db.evil.com'}  | 'postgresql://localhost/x?host=db.evil.com'
```

This is a genuine parser differential. The function uses `urlparse().hostname` which returns `localhost`, accepts it, and **never tokenizes the query string**. But libpq, when given a URI, parses query parameters — and a `host=` query parameter **overrides** the URI's host. So libpq connects to `db.evil.com`.

Let me confirm libpq's actual precedence (does the query `host=` win over the URI authority `localhost`?). The `PQconninfoParse` output shows `host: db.evil.com` — query won. Let me verify this rigorously and probe variants.

input.content:
import sys, ctypes
from urllib.parse import urlparse
sys.path.insert(0, "/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src")
from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri, NonLocalEndpointError

lib = ctypes.CDLL("/opt/homebrew/lib/postgresql@17/libpq.dylib")
class O(ctypes.Structure):
    _fields_ = [("keyword",ctypes.c_char_p),("envvar",ctypes.c_char_p),
        ("compiled",ctypes.c_char_p),("val",ctypes.c_char_p),
        ("label",ctypes.c_char_p),("dispchar",ctypes.c_char_p),("dispsize",ctypes.c_int)]
lib.PQconninfoParse.restype = ctypes.POINTER(O)
lib.PQconninfoParse.argtypes=[ctypes.c_char_p, ctypes.POINTER(ctypes.c_char_p)]
def libpq_target(dsn):
    e=ctypes.c_char_p(); o=lib.PQconninfoParse(dsn.encode(), ctypes.byref(e))
    if not o: return ("ERR", (e.value or b'').decode().strip())
    r={}; i=0
    while o[i].keyword:
        k=o[i].keyword.decode()
        if k in("host","hostaddr","port","dbname","user") and o[i].val: r[k]=o[i].val.decode()
        i+=1
    return r
def check(uri,note=""):
    try: require_local_uri(uri,what="x"); v="ACCEPT"
    except NonLocalEndpointError: v="REJECT"
    except Exception as e: v=f"ERR({type(e).__name__})"
    print(f"[{v:7}] urlparse.host={urlparse(uri).hostname!r:18} libpq={libpq_target(uri)} | {uri!r} {note}")

# Confirm the differential and find variants
for u,n in [
 ("postgresql://localhost/x?host=db.evil.com","CONFIRMED differential"),
 ("postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb?host=db.evil.com&port=5432","full-looking dsn"),
 ("postgresql://localhost/x?hostaddr=8.8.8.8","hostaddr via query"),
 ("postgresql://localhost/x?host=db.evil.com&sslmode=disable","with extra param"),
 ("postgres://127.0.0.1/x?host=evil.com","127.0.0.1 authority + query"),
 ("postgresql://[::1]/x?host=evil.com","ipv6 loopback + query host"),
 ("postgresql://localhost/x?host=evil.com&host=localhost","query host repeated"),
 ("postgresql://[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/db?host=db.evil.com","userinfo+port+query"),
]:
    check(u,n)

content.text:
Confirmed and robust. The bypass is fully reproducible. This is exactly the "parser differential" class the task flagged as highest-value.

Let me verify the precise libpq semantics from the spec: per the PostgreSQL connection URI documentation, **"Any connection parameters not corresponding to key words listed in Section 34.1.2 are ignored... It is possible to specify additional configuration parameters in the URI's query string."** And critically, for the `host` parameter specifically: when a URI contains both an authority host and a `host` query parameter, libpq's `conninfo_uri_parse` overrides. My ground-truth `PQconninfoParse` (the exact function libpq uses internally before connecting) confirms `host=db.evil.com` wins. So `psycopg.connect("postgresql://localhost/x?host=db.evil.com")` connects to **db.evil.com**.

Let me also double check the special pg case where authority is empty but a deeper differential exists, and verify the `hostaddr` query case (hostaddr=8.8.8.8 overrides host for the actual TCP target even when host=localhost stays as the TLS/SNI name).

input.content:
import sys, ctypes
sys.path.insert(0,"/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src")
from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri, NonLocalEndpointError
lib=ctypes.CDLL("/opt/homebrew/lib/postgresql@17/libpq.dylib")
class O(ctypes.Structure):
    _fields_=[("keyword",ctypes.c_char_p),("envvar",ctypes.c_char_p),("compiled",ctypes.c_char_p),
        ("val",ctypes.c_char_p),("label",ctypes.c_char_p),("dispchar",ctypes.c_char_p),("dispsize",ctypes.c_int)]
lib.PQconninfoParse.restype=ctypes.POINTER(O)
lib.PQconninfoParse.argtypes=[ctypes.c_char_p,ctypes.POINTER(ctypes.c_char_p)]
def tgt(d):
    e=ctypes.c_char_p();o=lib.PQconninfoParse(d.encode(),ctypes.byref(e))
    if not o:return("ERR",(e.value or b'').decode())
    r={};i=0
    while o[i].keyword:
        k=o[i].keyword.decode()
        if k in("host","hostaddr")and o[i].val:r[k]=o[i].val.decode()
        i+=1
    return r
# hostaddr is the ACTUAL ip libpq dials when present; host becomes only the SNI/auth name
for u in ["postgresql://localhost/x?hostaddr=8.8.8.8",
          "postgresql://localhost/x?host=evil.com&hostaddr=8.8.8.8"]:
    try: require_local_uri(u,what="x");v="ACCEPT"
    except NonLocalEndpointError:v="REJECT"
    print(v, u, "->", tgt(u))

Both accepted, both dial 8.8.8.8 (`hostaddr` is the literal TCP target libpq connects to; `host=localhost` only supplies the name for TLS/auth). This is an even cleaner remote bypass — the TCP connection goes straight to 8.8.8.8.

I have a definitive finding. Let me clean up temp files.

## Break-test result: GENUINE BYPASS FOUND

Target: `require_local_uri` in `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_local.py` (branch `feat/persistence-port`, head `4233f5b`).

I ran ~50 real repros against the live function and validated the connect-target with **real libpq** (`PQconninfoParse` from `/opt/homebrew/lib/postgresql@17/libpq.dylib` via ctypes — the exact parser libpq runs before dialing). The prior two fixes hardened the *DSN tokenizer path* (whitespace/quoting), but the **URI path was never reconciled with libpq's URI semantics**. That's the gap.

### FINDING 1 — CRITICAL — URI query-string `host`/`hostaddr` parser differential

The function does `host = urlparse(uri).hostname`. For a `postgresql://` URI it gets the **authority** host, sees `localhost`, accepts, and returns — it **never inspects the query string**. But libpq, given the same URI, treats query parameters as connection keywords, and a `host=`/`hostaddr=` query parameter **overrides the authority host**. So `urlparse` and libpq disagree about the host, and the guard trusts the wrong one.

Confirmed accepted-but-remote inputs (function verdict / real libpq target):

| Input | Function | libpq actually dials |
|---|---|---|
| `postgresql://localhost/x?host=db.evil.com` | **ACCEPT** | `host=db.evil.com` |
| `postgresql://localhost/x?hostaddr=8.8.8.8` | **ACCEPT** | TCP → `8.8.8.8` (host=localhost is only the TLS/auth name) |
| `postgresql://localhost/x?host=evil.com&hostaddr=8.8.8.8` | **ACCEPT** | TCP → `8.8.8.8`, SNI `evil.com` |
| `postgres://127.0.0.1/x?host=evil.com` | **ACCEPT** | `host=evil.com` |
| `postgresql://[::1]/x?host=evil.com` | **ACCEPT** | `host=evil.com` |
| `postgresql://[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/db?host=db.evil.com` | **ACCEPT** | `host=db.evil.com` |

Why it's remote: `PQconninfoParse` (the literal function libpq calls before connecting) resolves these to the remote host/hostaddr. With `hostaddr=8.8.8.8` the TCP socket connects directly to 8.8.8.8 — a clean ADR-0003 data-exfiltration path that passes the guard. (psycopg/psycopg2 aren't installed in the venv, but they wrap this same libpq, so the behavior is identical.)

Severity CRITICAL: the most plausible store endpoint (the checkpoint Postgres DSN this helper exists to protect) can be pointed off-box while the guard reports "local."

### SUGGESTED FIX
When the URI parses to a host (the `if host:` branch), also parse and check the query string for `host`/`hostaddr`, the same way the DSN branch already checks tokens. Concretely, after confirming the authority host is loopback, run `urllib.parse.parse_qs(urlparse(uri).query)` and reject if any `host` or `hostaddr` value is non-loopback. Equivalently/more robustly: for `postgres(ql)://` URIs, don't trust `urlparse` at all — feed the string through a libpq-faithful parser (or `PQconninfoParse`) and validate every resolved `host` **and** `hostaddr`. Note `hostaddr` is the literal IP libpq dials and must be checked independently of `host` (a loopback `host` does **not** make a remote `hostaddr` safe).

### Classes I tried that are SAFE (correctly rejected)
- Repeated `host=` DSN keys (`host=localhost host=db.evil.com` and reverse): rejected — the tokenizer checks **all** matches, so libpq's last-wins can't sneak past.
- DSN whitespace/tabs/newline around `=`, mixed-case keys, single/double quotes, escaped quotes, backslash-escaped spaces, `host` not first: all rejected.
- DSN `hostaddr` (with empty/loopback `host`, or alone): rejected — both `host` and `hostaddr` are in the keyword set.
- IP obfuscation in DSN/URI (`2130706433`, `0x7f000001`, `017700000001`, `0.0.0.0`): rejected (these aren't loopback per `ipaddress`, and that matches libpq, which doesn't decode them to 127.0.0.1 either).
- Prefix/lookalike hosts (`127.0.0.1.evil.com`, `localhost.evil.com`, trailing-dot `localhost.`): rejected.
- IPv6 `[::1]` and `[::ffff:127.0.0.1]` accepted (genuinely loopback); `[::ffff:1.2.3.4]` rejected — correct.
- `sqlite://host`, scheme-relative `//host`: the URI path catches the smuggled host and rejects.
- Null-byte injection in DSN: rejected.

The single broken class is the URI-with-query differential above.
